items.prompt_md: In a flood-fill DFS over a grid, why must a cell be marked visited at the moment DFS steps onto it, rather than only when its neighbors are examined?
options: So the same cell can never be pushed onto the call stack a second time — the 'already visited' guard has to fire before another path reaches it
So the total area of the island can be printed to the console for debugging
So the grid is converted into a directed graph
So the recursion always terminates after exactly one call
items.answer: So the same cell can never be pushed onto the call stack a second time — the 'already visited' guard has to fire before another path reaches it
items.explanation_md: If a cell isn't marked until its neighbors are checked, several neighboring cells can each queue up a call into it before any of them marks it, so it gets revisited or double-counted.

items.prompt_md: You're asked to flood-fill a grid but must not mutate the input grid itself. What replaces `grid[r][c] = "sunk"` as the visited marker?
options: A separate visited set (or same-shape boolean grid) checked and updated alongside the traversal
A stack of previously returned function calls
Sorting the grid rows before traversal
A running total of every cell's value
items.answer: A separate visited set (or same-shape boolean grid) checked and updated alongside the traversal
items.explanation_md: When the input can't be mutated in place, an auxiliary `visited` set/array plays the exact same role the in-place marker did — checked before entering a cell, set the moment you enter it.

items.prompt_md: A DFS flood fill marks a cell visited only *after* recursing into all four of its neighbors, instead of before. What goes wrong?
options: The same cell can be re-entered by more than one neighbor before it's ever marked, causing infinite recursion or repeated counting
Nothing — marking order never affects correctness, only performance
The grid's dimensions become inconsistent
DFS silently converts into BFS
items.answer: The same cell can be re-entered by more than one neighbor before it's ever marked, causing infinite recursion or repeated counting
items.explanation_md: Marking has to happen on entry, not on exit — if a cell isn't flagged until after its own recursive calls return, two different neighbors can each call into it first, and the recursion never bottoms out.

items.prompt_md: Grid adjacency defined as "shares an up/down/left/right edge" makes the grid an undirected graph. Why?
options: Because if cell A is adjacent to cell B, cell B is automatically adjacent to cell A — the relationship has no direction
Because every cell has exactly two neighbors
Because DFS only works on undirected graphs
Because the grid has no cycles
items.answer: Because if cell A is adjacent to cell B, cell B is automatically adjacent to cell A — the relationship has no direction
items.explanation_md: Adjacency here is symmetric — "A borders B" and "B borders A" are the same fact — which is exactly the definition of an undirected edge.

items.prompt_md: In the outer double loop that scans every cell of the grid, what does incrementing a counter each time it finds an unvisited land cell actually count?
options: The number of separate connected components (islands) in the grid
The total number of land cells
The perimeter of the largest island
The number of times DFS recurses
items.answer: The number of separate connected components (islands) in the grid
items.explanation_md: Each time the scan lands on a cell that hasn't been swallowed by an earlier flood fill, that cell must belong to a brand-new component — so the counter increments once per component, not once per cell.

items.prompt_md: A grid flood fill is implemented with recursive DFS. On a grid containing one enormous, snake-shaped island, what risk does this introduce that an iterative (explicit-stack) DFS avoids?
options: A stack overflow, because the recursion depth can grow as large as the number of cells in the island
An infinite loop, because recursion never terminates on grids
Incorrect island counts, because recursion double-counts cells
A directed cycle being introduced into the grid
items.answer: A stack overflow, because the recursion depth can grow as large as the number of cells in the island
items.explanation_md: Each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack, so a single island snaking through most of the grid can recurse as deep as the island is large — swapping in an explicit stack moves that memory onto the heap instead of the call stack.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of counting all islands in an R×C grid with flood fill?
options: O(R × C)
O(R + C)
O(R × C × log(R × C))
items.answer: O(R × C)
items.explanation_md: The outer scan visits every cell once, and because each cell is marked visited the instant it's entered, every cell is also flood-filled at most once — so the total work is proportional to the number of cells.

items.prompt_md: What is the worst-case space complexity of the recursive flood fill on an R×C grid?
options: O(R × C)
O(log(R × C))
O(R + C)
items.answer: O(R × C)
items.explanation_md: If a single island snakes through nearly every cell, the recursion (or an explicit visited set) can hold on the order of R×C entries at once.

items.prompt_md: A problem defines two cells as adjacent only if they share an up/down/left/right edge. What's wrong with a solution that also treats diagonal neighbors as connected?
options: It changes which cells count as part of the same component, so it can merge islands the problem considers separate
Nothing — diagonal adjacency is always equivalent to 4-directional adjacency
It would make the algorithm run in O(1) time
It converts the flood fill from DFS into topological sort
items.answer: It changes which cells count as part of the same component, so it can merge islands the problem considers separate
items.explanation_md: Connectivity is defined by the problem's adjacency rule, not by geometry in general — adding diagonal neighbors the problem didn't specify can silently fuse two separate islands into one.

items.prompt_md: Why does the flood fill need to track visited status per individual cell, rather than just per connected component?
options: Because the traversal is what discovers which cells belong to a component in the first place — there's no way to know a cell's component ahead of time without visiting it
Because each cell can belong to more than one component at once
Because per-component tracking uses less memory
Because the grid is always a complete graph
items.answer: Because the traversal is what discovers which cells belong to a component in the first place — there's no way to know a cell's component ahead of time without visiting it
items.explanation_md: You don't know a cell belongs to a given island until DFS actually reaches it, so each individual cell must be marked as it's discovered — that's the only way to avoid revisiting or re-counting cells already swept into the current flood fill.

items.prompt_md: A grid marks each cell with a color id. You need to count how many maximal regions of same-colored, 4-directionally-adjacent cells exist. Which approach fits?
options: DFS/BFS flood fill from each unvisited cell, marking cells visited as you go
BFS for shortest unweighted path between two specific cells
Topological sort by in-degree over the cells
Two-pointer scan over the flattened grid
items.answer: DFS/BFS flood fill from each unvisited cell, marking cells visited as you go
items.explanation_md: "Count maximal connected regions" is a reachability question, not a shortest-path or ordering question — flood fill from each unvisited cell is exactly how you enumerate connected components.

items.prompt_md: You have a floor plan grid where 0 = open floor and 1 = wall. Given one starting room, determine whether a specific target room is reachable at all (the number of steps doesn't matter). Which approach fits?
options: DFS (or BFS) from the start, marking visited rooms, and checking whether the target is ever reached
Multi-source BFS to compute the minimum time for something to spread
Union-Find merging all rooms pairwise
Backtracking to enumerate every possible path
items.answer: DFS (or BFS) from the start, marking visited rooms, and checking whether the target is ever reached
items.explanation_md: When the question is purely "can I reach it," not "how fast," a single traversal that marks visited cells is enough — no need for level tracking or exhaustive path enumeration.

items.prompt_md: In a binary grid, you must find which land cells are NOT already connected to the border, then flip only those to water. Which approach fits?
options: Flood-fill (DFS/BFS) starting from every border land cell to mark "safe" cells, then treat everything unmarked as enclosed
Kahn's algorithm to topologically order the cells
A min-heap to repeatedly pop the smallest cell value
Two pointers converging from opposite grid corners
items.answer: Flood-fill (DFS/BFS) starting from every border land cell to mark "safe" cells, then treat everything unmarked as enclosed
items.explanation_md: The trick is to flood-fill outward from the border first — whatever a border-seeded flood fill never touches must be interior, giving you the enclosed regions without ever exploring "from the inside out."

items.prompt_md: Given a pixel grid and a starting pixel, you must repaint every pixel of the same original color that's reachable via 4-directional steps from that pixel. Which approach fits?
options: DFS/BFS flood fill from the starting pixel with a visited/repainted check
Sort all pixels by color then binary search for the target color
A monotonic stack over the pixel values
Fast/slow pointers to detect a repeating pattern
items.answer: DFS/BFS flood fill from the starting pixel with a visited/repainted check
items.explanation_md: This is the canonical flood-fill shape: repaint (mark visited) the instant you arrive at a matching pixel, then spread to its same-colored neighbors.

items.prompt_md: You're given an adjacency list for an undirected graph (not a grid) and must count how many connected components it has. Which approach fits?
options: DFS/BFS from each unvisited node, counting how many traversals it takes to cover every node
BFS seeded from every node simultaneously to compute pairwise distances
Topological sort, since components must be processed in dependency order
A sliding window over the node list
items.answer: DFS/BFS from each unvisited node, counting how many traversals it takes to cover every node
items.explanation_md: Connected-component counting generalizes directly from grids to general graphs: launch a fresh traversal from each unvisited node and count how many traversals you needed.

items.prompt_md: You have a sorted array of distinct integers and a target sum, and must find two numbers that add up to the target. Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers starting from opposite ends and moving inward based on the current sum
DFS flood fill with a visited set
Multi-source BFS from every array index
Topological sort of the array values
items.answer: Two pointers starting from opposite ends and moving inward based on the current sum
items.explanation_md: There's no grid or graph here — a sorted array plus a target-sum pair is the textbook opposite-ends two-pointer setup, not a traversal problem.

items.prompt_md: You need to group a list of strings so that all anagrams of each other end up in the same group. Which approach fits?
options: A hash map keyed by each string's sorted letters (or letter-frequency signature)
DFS flood fill over the strings treated as grid cells
BFS layer-by-layer over the strings
Topological sort of the strings by dependency
items.answer: A hash map keyed by each string's sorted letters (or letter-frequency signature)
items.explanation_md: Grouping by a derived key is a hashing problem, not a graph traversal — there's no adjacency or connectivity between the strings at all.

items.prompt_md: You have an n×n adjacency matrix showing direct friendships between people, and must count how many separate friend groups exist (a friend of a friend counts as the same group). Which approach fits?
options: DFS/BFS traversal from each unvisited person, marking everyone reachable as the same group
A max-heap tracking the most-connected person
Binary search over the number of friendships
A monotonic stack of people ordered by friend count
items.answer: DFS/BFS traversal from each unvisited person, marking everyone reachable as the same group
items.explanation_md: "Friend of a friend is the same group" is exactly transitive connectivity — traverse and mark reachable nodes from each unvisited person, same as flood-filling islands.

items.prompt_md: Given an array, you must find the maximum value in every contiguous window of size k as the window slides across the array. Which approach fits?
options: A monotonic deque that keeps only candidates for the current window's maximum
DFS flood fill over the array indices
Multi-source BFS seeded from every window
Union-Find merging adjacent windows
items.answer: A monotonic deque that keeps only candidates for the current window's maximum
items.explanation_md: Sliding-window maximum is solved with a monotonic deque (a stack-family structure), not a grid/graph traversal — there's no notion of visiting neighboring cells here.

items.prompt_md: A grid problem asks: "how many islands are there, where an island is a maximal group of 1s connected 4-directionally?" Which approach fits?
options: DFS/BFS flood fill from each unvisited land cell, marking visited cells so no island is double-counted
BFS to find the shortest path between two islands
Topological sort of the land cells by dependency
A min-heap to repeatedly extract the smallest unvisited cell
items.answer: DFS/BFS flood fill from each unvisited land cell, marking visited cells so no island is double-counted
items.explanation_md: This is the direct restatement of the lesson's core pattern: count connected components in a grid by flood-filling from each unvisited land cell.
